What do you need them to do?
What's the outcomes of the role?
As one design engineer role was very much a product designer who knows code and doesn't rely on, like, the vibe coding, like, Lovable, Cursor, V0.
There was another one where they, that's all they, they wanted them familiar with it, but it was more of a designer that could vibe code a bit here and there.
Then there was another.
The one which was almost more of like, I would class it as an interactive developer where they were coding using like WebGL for like visual experiences.
So each role was very different from that standpoint.
But I'm curious, I guess, when you're kind of looking for these design engineers, I know there's a lot of people that because of Vibe Coding, they're now classing themselves as design engineers.
Then there's other people that have been coding and rapid prototyping and designing for a long time.
And now this helps more.
Well, and they've always been design engineers, what is it you kind of look for when you're hiring that?
Yeah, it depends on the area.
I think, you know, so for, so we have two, we split the company in two areas, right?
Deliver and build.
Build is essentially the engineering product and design org.
So design engineering, if you will, engineers.
And we just hired our first PM, mostly focused on, mostly focused on.

And on the deliver side, we have a range of agencies that support us with design engineering support as well, but geared more towards, yeah, I'd say, you know, it's like the visual side, website of things.

The deliver side is definitely geared more towards design engineering, and in all honesty, it's like, I'm still wrapping my head around, it's like overall process, and what good process, or it's like what good ways of working, you know, like, means in this day and age, specifically.
In addition to, you know, it's like the overall, the overall skill set that we're going to require on there.

On long-term, but on short-term, know, the types of design engineers that will be successful for us are definitely, I'd say, you know, like, and there's not many like them, but Jump Hero archetype, you know, like, folks that have ideally started in engineering becomes, like, very proficient around front-end specifically, and through that, gotten, you know, like, ideally deeply in touch with design.
But really, you know, like, almost from that engineering perspective, it doesn't mean that, you know, it's like there's no, there are no, you know, like, designers design skills, if you will.
But it does gear more towards, you know, it's like a full stack towards front-end engineers with very solid design skills or AI.
So where we are right now, in terms of product development, we're still at a phase where we use off-the-shelf chatty and components.
There's very little custom work still happening because we're still carrying out, it's like many of the core Chrome for the product.
We're validating plenty of features.

So I think one of the biggest challenges would be folks that have some of that base, but at the same time have, I'd say, a knowledge for interaction design, or a understanding of a logical architecture for a system.
In a decent way.
It's also fairly complex, the moving pieces that we need to figure out and tie together.
So designers that are able to maintain a 10,000 feet perspective of the overall system and how their work fits into all of the other moving pieces at that moment.
And I was able to zoom in a bit.
That, I think, is the biggest intellectual challenge within the work right now.

But when you look at design engineering for the build side as compared to many of the design leaders that I speak to and what they're hiring for right now specifically enter their product design orgs.
um
We centralize the visual talent more in the labor org right now and prioritize that for clients, essentially.

Is it ready to be out and have our users seeing this and it been reflecting the brand?
And then the build can be a little bit more bigger problem thinking.
It's something that I think, especially when you've got a big product.

Similar to like Rippling was in as an offering for the HR world.

Big problem spaces of an interaction design mindset, which it's always a little bit more of a tougher skill.
So I think there's a lot of great consumer products that look great out there.
And then when it comes down to it, they just haven't got the complexity.

I think he used an example of, you in some problem spaces, especially in more like software space, it's a lot more around, you can't, it's not a matter of simplification upon simplification, like consumer gradient.
So you need to have somebody who can think on that complexity, but then dive in as well at the right point.
The coding bit is definitely something we're seeing a lot more of as well.
And it's fascinating just kind of where, how they use that as well.
I think some companies are saying, they want to hire a design engineer.
And I'm like, you don't really need a design engineer.
You can get a product designer that knows code or knows vibe coding enough.
Whereas others, I'm like, oh, I can see why you need it.
Because it's very like tailored and it's not as simple.
Kind of a, you know, it's beyond what some of the vibe coding can do.
So it's very interesting kind of seeing that blend.
And those types of candidates are quite few and far between.

It's like I've seen most designers be so inherently creative that they can adopt, you know, it's like any new tool fairly quickly and any new tool set that a company uses fairly quickly.

I think it's, know, it's like, how do they, so we're fully remote, so it's really important that folks are, you it's like very proactive in communication and don't, you know, sit around and wait and do a lot of their own project management and be really autonomous and self-driven.
I think that that, you know, it's like a lot of folks think and think they want to be, they want to be that way or want to be perceived that way.
But not necessarily always reality.
So that remote culture communication bubble really needs to be present there.
